topic: A person runs into their ex at a mutual friend's wedding
raw_output:
[story 1]

The string quartet played a delicate waltz, the air thick with expensive perfume and forced merriment. I was nursing a lukewarm champagne flute, trying to focus on the intricate pattern of the tablecloth, when I saw him. Standing near the buffet, laughing easily with a group of older acquaintances, was Alex. My breath hitched, a sudden, sharp constriction in my chest. Every muscle in my jaw tightened. I pretended to be deeply engrossed in a conversation with a distant cousin, my gaze darting sideways, hoping he wouldn't notice the way my knuckles were white against the glass. When he finally turned, our eyes met across the crowded room. A flicker—a recognition so sharp it felt like a physical jolt—passed between us before he offered a polite, distant nod and moved on. I felt a sudden, desperate need to melt into the wallpaper.

[story 2]

“Look who decided to grace us with their presence,” Sarah chirped, nudging me forward. I managed a tight smile, the kind that doesn't reach my eyes. Then I saw her. Across the dance floor, bathed in the warm glow of the string lights, was Maya. She was stunning, undeniably so, and the sight of her triggered a peculiar clenching sensation low in my stomach. I excused myself abruptly, claiming I needed air, and navigated the periphery of the room, moving with exaggerated purpose. Each step felt heavy, deliberate. When I finally reached the edge of the patio, leaning against a stone pillar, I watched her laugh with someone else. The sound seemed too loud, too bright. I gripped the cool stone, trying to anchor myself against the strange, fluttering anxiety that had taken root in my core.

[story 3]

The music shifted to something more energetic, and the noise level rose. I was trying to appear relaxed, sipping my wine, but my focus kept snagging on him. Liam. He was talking animatedly to the groom, his gestures fluid and confident. I watched him from across the room, my attention snagged like a loose thread. My hands began to tremble almost imperceptibly beneath the table. I consciously slowed my breathing, trying to regulate the frantic rhythm inside me. When he glanced over, our eyes briefly locked. There was a moment—a silent acknowledgment that felt heavy with unspoken history—before he broke the gaze, turning back to his conversation. I quickly lowered my hand, forcing my fingers to remain still on the stem of my glass, feeling a distinct pressure building behind my eyes. I needed to leave. Now.
